findings.title: Comprehensive 15+ format support with optional dependencies
findings.summary: MarkItDown supports PDF, DOCX, PPTX, XLSX, images (with EXIF/OCR), audio (with transcription), HTML, YouTube URLs, CSV, JSON, XML, EPubs, ZIP, and Azure Document Intelligence integration.
findings.invalidation_condition: Removal of format from supported list
findings.decay_rationale: Format support is architectural and unlikely to decrease; backward compatibility incentivizes maintenance.

findings.title: Optimized for LLM consumption: 90% token savings vs HTML
findings.summary: MarkItDown prioritizes semantic structure and token efficiency for AI consumption. Markdown headings require 3 tokens vs 23 in HTML. Design explicitly targets language model ingestion.
findings.invalidation_condition: Evidence that LLM Markdown comprehension is no longer an advantage (unlikely)
findings.decay_rationale: LLM native Markdown support is foundational and stable; token efficiency is intrinsic to format.

findings.title: 100x faster than Docling but 25% PDF accuracy on complex documents
findings.summary: MarkItDown achieves 180+ files/sec throughput; Docling is slower. However, PDF conversion success rate is ~25% for complex PDFs; Docling achieves 97.9% accuracy on structured table extraction.
findings.invalidation_condition: New MarkItDown algorithm achieves >90% accuracy on complex PDFs without sacrificing speed
findings.decay_rationale: Accuracy metrics are actively developing as tools iterate; PDF processing is rapid-innovation domain.

findings.title: Table extraction uses column-wise enumeration, destroying row-column relationships
findings.summary: Tables are extracted column-by-column rather than row-by-row, rendering complex tables unusable. Example: Transaction table (Date, Desc, Amount) becomes [all dates], [all descriptions], [all amounts]. Affects PDF, DOCX, PPTX, XLSX.
findings.invalidation_condition: Table extraction algorithm rewritten to preserve row-column relationships
findings.decay_rationale: Column-wise enumeration is architectural; Markdown's native table syntax is the root cause, unlikely to change.

findings.title: OCR and enhanced image extraction require external LLM integration
findings.summary: Core tool has basic image handling; text extraction from images and OCR for scanned documents require external LLM (GPT-4o, Claude) via optional markitdown-ocr plugin.
findings.invalidation_condition: Native OCR integration added to core MarkItDown without external API calls
findings.decay_rationale: Plugin architecture is design choice; unlikely to change to built-in OCR.

findings.title: Recommended for RAG pipelines (scope-dependent: simple vs complex documents)
findings.summary: MarkItDown recommended for simple-text RAG pipelines (legal docs, plain-text papers). Not recommended for structured RAG requiring accurate table extraction (Docling superior at 97.9% accuracy).
findings.invalidation_condition: Evidence that MarkItDown outperforms Docling on structured RAG or vice versa
findings.decay_rationale: RAG tool recommendations are rapidly evolving; new tools and benchmarks emerge frequently.

findings.title: MCP server integration enables agent automation (29+ formats)
findings.summary: MarkItDown MCP server (markitdown-mcp) exposes document conversion as tool for Claude Desktop and MCP-compatible agents. Multiple implementations available (trsdn, KorigamiK). Deployable via Docker.
findings.invalidation_condition: MCP server disconnected or deprecated
findings.decay_rationale: MCP is foundational standard; unlikely to deprecate. MarkItDown ecosystem participation is stable.

findings.title: Growing plugin ecosystem disabled by default for security
findings.summary: MarkItDown supports plugins (markitdown-ocr, Korean HWP, web scraping). Disabled by default; activated with simple flag. Avoids bloat and security risks.
findings.invalidation_condition: Plugin ecosystem deprecated or abandoned
findings.decay_rationale: Community plugin adoption is unpredictable; third-party ecosystem can stall or accelerate rapidly.

findings.title: Known limitations: scanned PDFs, HTML regressions, stream conversion
findings.summary: Scanned/protected PDFs produce blank output. HTML-to-Markdown regressions after markdownify updates cause raw HTML passthrough. convert_stream() requires binary file-like objects, not text streams.
findings.invalidation_condition: Scanned PDF handling implemented or HTML regression fixed
findings.decay_rationale: Known edge case limitations; fixes may come with updates, but design limitations (scanned PDFs lack text layer) are persistent.

findings.title: MIT license, 91k stars, strong adoption and maintenance
findings.summary: 91,000 GitHub stars, 5,400 forks, 74 contributors in ~6 months. MIT license removes commercial friction. Integration with AutoGen and Azure signaling strategic positioning.
findings.invalidation_condition: Major license change or organizational deprioritization
findings.decay_rationale: Adoption metrics and organizational backing are unlikely to reverse; MIT license is permanent.

findings.title: Synchronous PDFMiner architecture causes severe degradation on large files (>10MB)
findings.summary: PDFMiner.extract_text is completely synchronous. Performance degrades from 35 files/sec to 60+ minutes per file on documents >10MB. Single GitHub issue (#1276) and independent benchmark document this.
findings.invalidation_condition: Async PDFMiner wrapper or native async extraction implemented
findings.decay_rationale: PDFMiner is externally maintained; async improvements could arrive at any time.

findings.title: Encoding/Unicode crashes on non-ASCII characters (Cyrillic, CJK, special Unicode)
findings.summary: Tool crashes with UnicodeEncodeError or produces garbled output on Cyrillic, CJK, special symbols (©, ™, ✓). Multiple GitHub issues (#138, #291, #1290). Does not gracefully skip problematic characters.
findings.invalidation_condition: Unicode handling refactored to support all character sets gracefully
findings.decay_rationale: Character encoding is architectural; fixes depend on dependency updates and core refactoring.

findings.title: CVE-2025-64512: RCE via malicious PDF in pdfminer.six <20251107
findings.summary: pdfminer.six <20251107 uses pickle.loads() for CMap deserialization. Malicious PDF can redirect pickle.gz loading for remote code execution. MarkItDown v0.1.3 vulnerable before Dec 2025 patch (v0.1.4).
findings.invalidation_condition: pdfminer.six >20251107 fully eliminates vulnerability (unlikely; GHSA-f83h-ghpp-7wcc proved incomplete)
findings.decay_rationale: CVE history is immutable; historical vulnerability is permanent fact.

findings.title: CRITICAL: GHSA-f83h-ghpp-7wcc is DISTINCT from CVE-2025-64512
findings.summary: GHSA-f83h-ghpp-7wcc (CVE-2025-70559) is separate insecure deserialization vulnerability. First patch (CVE-2025-64512) did not address it. Local privilege escalation via pickle CMap files in writable directories.
findings.invalidation_condition: Vulnerabilities proven to be identical (they are not; advisories explicitly distinct)
findings.decay_rationale: Security advisory history is immutable.

findings.title: CRITICAL: MarkItDown v0.1.5 does NOT patch GHSA-f83h-ghpp-7wcc
findings.summary: MarkItDown v0.1.5 pins pdfminer.six 20251107 to patch CVE-2025-64512. However, GHSA-f83h-ghpp-7wcc requires pdfminer.six >=20251230 (replaces pickle with JSON). Current v0.1.5 is unpatched for second vulnerability.
findings.invalidation_condition: MarkItDown v0.1.6+ upgrades pdfminer.six to 20251230 or later
findings.decay_rationale: Dependency pins are facts; vulnerability status is immutable until patched.

findings.title: DOCX structure preservation is PARTIAL: merged cells and nested tables lost
findings.summary: Landscape claims DOCX converts to 'proper Markdown tables with structure preservation.' Reality: Merged cells not handled (GitHub Issue #20), nested tables completely discarded (GitHub Issue #1248). Docling significantly outperforms MarkItDown on DOCX conversion.
findings.invalidation_condition: DOCX table conversion algorithm rewritten to support merged cells and nested tables
findings.decay_rationale: Root cause is Markdown table syntax limitation and python-docx library constraints; fixes require upstream changes.

findings.title: Nested tables in DOCX files are completely discarded during conversion
findings.summary: When DOCX contains nested tables, outer table structure is preserved but inner table content is missing. Tool does not flatten or preserve nested tables as HTML alternatives; it simply discards them.
findings.invalidation_condition: Nested table handling implemented in MarkItDown or python-docx
findings.decay_rationale: Feature request depends on upstream library support; unlikely without major refactor.

findings.title: Installation is simple but carries 25 transitive dependencies with hidden operational costs
findings.summary: Installation via pip is straightforward. However, 25 transitive dependencies require security patching (CVE-2025-64512 required urgent patching). Production use adds encoding error handling (15-20% code complexity), retry logic, and dependency version pinning.
findings.invalidation_condition: Dependency tree reduced significantly or dependency security incidents cease
findings.decay_rationale: OSS dependency risk is structural and persistent; unlikely to improve without architectural change.

findings.title: Wrapper library architecture limits quality ceiling to underlying dependencies
findings.summary: MarkItDown is thin wrapper around pdfminer (text-only extraction), python-docx (no merged cell support), python-pptx (incomplete). Quality ceiling for each format determined by underlying library limitations, not MarkItDown design.
findings.invalidation_condition: Native PDF/DOCX/PPTX parsing engines implemented in place of wrapper pattern
findings.decay_rationale: Wrapper architecture is design choice; unlikely to change without complete rewrite.

findings.title: PDF conversion success rate ~25% on complex/structured documents
findings.summary: Benchmarks show MarkItDown breaks simple text lines, loses heading levels, destroys table structure on complex PDFs. Success rate ~25% on structured PDFs. Docling (97.9%) and Marker (good) preserve structure; Mistral Document AI also superior.
findings.invalidation_condition: PDF conversion algorithm rewritten to preserve heading levels and table structure
findings.decay_rationale: PDF processing is rapidly evolving; algorithm improvements could arrive with new versions.

findings.title: PPTX conversion has documented failures but NO quantitative benchmarks
findings.summary: GitHub issues document PPTX crashes (TypeError: NoneType vs Emu, Issue #1293), invalid file error handling (Issue #1408), missing image extraction (Issue #56). No independent benchmarks comparing PPTX conversion accuracy to Docling or Marker.
findings.invalidation_condition: Independent PPTX benchmark study published comparing MarkItDown to alternatives
findings.decay_rationale: Quality metrics for PPTX conversion do not exist; benchmarking could change assessment significantly.

findings.title: XLSX conversion quality is undocumented
findings.summary: Landscape brief claims XLSX support. Zero quality data available (no benchmarks, no GitHub issue analysis, no comparative testing). Feature claimed but unvalidated.
findings.invalidation_condition: Independent XLSX conversion test published; quality metrics documented
findings.decay_rationale: Complete absence of evidence; any new benchmark could shift assessment.

findings.title: Regional/non-Latin language support quality undocumented
findings.summary: Encoding crashes documented for Cyrillic, CJK, special Unicode. Scope of language support unclear—no testing on Chinese, Arabic, Japanese, or other non-Latin scripts beyond crash reports.
findings.invalidation_condition: Multilingual document test suite published with success rates per language
findings.decay_rationale: Crash reports indicate failures, but overall support quality is completely unmapped.

findings.title: Azure Document Intelligence integration cost-performance at scale undocumented
findings.summary: Deep-Dive mentions Azure integration ($1-5/page) as fallback for complex PDFs. No TCO analysis, cost-performance benchmarks, or hybrid pipeline cost data available. True cost of 'simple' MarkItDown + Azure tier unknown.
findings.invalidation_condition: Hybrid MarkItDown + Azure TCO study published; cost per page and accuracy metrics documented
findings.decay_rationale: Azure pricing/performance is unknown; future pricing or tool changes could shift analysis.

findings.title: Docling-to-MarkItDown production migration rate undocumented
findings.summary: No data on how many production systems migrated from MarkItDown to Docling or alternatives post-CVE-2025-64512. Would indicate whether architectural limitations force tool replacement at scale.
findings.invalidation_condition: Industry survey or case study documenting tool migration patterns published
findings.decay_rationale: Migration data is proprietary and sparse; future surveys could reveal adoption trends.

findings.title: MarkItDown positioned as 'simple, lightweight' but marketing undersells operational complexity
findings.summary: Landscape and vendor blogs emphasize ease of installation and multi-format support. Marketing does not highlight CVE history, encoding fragility, table destruction, or hidden operational costs (dependency patching, error handling, fallback strategies).
findings.invalidation_condition: Vendor marketing updated to disclose limitations (unlikely)
findings.decay_rationale: Marketing bias is institutional; unlikely to change unless user backlash forces transparency.

must_carry_caveats.text: MarkItDown v0.1.5 contains an unpatched local privilege escalation vulnerability (GHSA-f83h-ghpp-7wcc / CVE-2025-70559) in the pdfminer.six dependency. The pinned version (20251107) addresses CVE-2025-64512 but NOT the subsequent GHSA-f83h-ghpp-7wcc vulnerability, which requires pdfminer.six >=20251230. Users must either manually upgrade pdfminer.six or wait for MarkItDown v0.1.6+ before deploying to production.
must_carry_caveats.reason: Critical security vulnerability in current stable release disqualifies blanket production recommendations without explicit mitigation planning.

must_carry_caveats.text: Table extraction uses column-wise enumeration rather than row-column preservation, rendering complex tables unusable for downstream analysis. This applies to PDF, DOCX, PPTX, and XLSX. Do not use MarkItDown for any pipeline requiring accurate table structure preservation or structured data extraction.
must_carry_caveats.reason: Architectural limitation; affects core use case and disqualifies entire document categories.

must_carry_caveats.text: MarkItDown crashes or produces garbled output on documents containing non-ASCII characters (Cyrillic, CJK, special Unicode symbols). Suitable for English-language documents only; non-English documents require pre-screening or tool replacement.
must_carry_caveats.reason: Character encoding failures render tool unsuitable for multilingual pipelines without additional error handling.

must_carry_caveats.text: RAG pipeline fit is scope-dependent: MarkItDown excels for simple-text RAG (legal documents, academic papers as plain text). For structured RAG requiring accurate table and layout preservation, Docling (97.9% accuracy) and Marker are superior alternatives.
must_carry_caveats.reason: Scope-dependent recommendation prevents over-broad adoption without context.

must_carry_caveats.text: Installation is simple (one pip command), but production operational complexity is significant: 25 transitive dependencies require ongoing security patching, encoding error handling adds 15-20% code complexity, and batch processing requires exponential backoff and fallback mechanisms.
must_carry_caveats.reason: Prevents underselling of hidden operational costs in production deployments.

must_carry_caveats.text: MarkItDown is a wrapper around existing libraries (pdfminer for PDF, python-docx for DOCX, python-pptx for PPTX). Quality ceiling for each format is determined by the underlying library's limitations, not MarkItDown's design. Specialized converters (Docling, Marker, MinerU) may outperform MarkItDown on specific formats.
must_carry_caveats.reason: Explains why MarkItDown cannot match specialized converters on accuracy, preventing false equivalence.

must_carry_caveats.text: DOCX and PPTX conversion exhibit structural failures: merged cells are lost in DOCX, nested tables are completely discarded, and PPTX files trigger crash errors on invalid documents. Multi-format support claim is overstated without these caveats.
must_carry_caveats.reason: Contradicts landscape brief's implicit claim that DOCX/PPTX are reliable alternatives to PDF.

must_carry_caveats.text: PPTX quantitative conversion accuracy metrics are not available in independent benchmarks. Quality assessment relies on GitHub issue reports of specific failures, not overall accuracy metrics. Do not make quantitative PPTX conversion claims without explicit caveat that no benchmarks exist.
must_carry_caveats.reason: Prevents false precision claims on undocumented quality.

material_findings: F9: Known limitations and regressions (slow decay—architectural)
F15: CRITICAL: Unpatched CVE status (volatile—depends on v0.1.6 release)
F20: PDF conversion success rate (fast decay)
F24: Azure integration cost-performance (volatile—pricing subject to change)
F25: Migration rate data (volatile—depends on industry surveys)
staleness_summary.fastest_material_decay: F3, F6, F11, F20 (fast / 6-month decay): Performance benchmarks, RAG recommendations, and conversion success rates are actively evolving in a competitive market with rapid iteration.
core_findings: F14-F15: CRITICAL CVE findings (stable but requires action)
volatile_findings: F15: CRITICAL unpatched CVE status (volatile—depends on v0.1.6 release timeline)
F21: PPTX quality (volatile—no benchmarks exist; any study changes assessment)
F24: Azure TCO (volatile—pricing subject to change)
F25: Migration patterns (volatile—proprietary data, sparse reports)
